narrative:
Karnataka High Court heard **1,096 of 3,215 scheduled matters**, an overall hearing rate of **34.1%** across **52 judges/benches**. That is lower than the recent comparable days in the retrieved context on **2026-04-17 (38.0%)** and **2026-04-16 (37.4%)**, though still clearly above **2026-04-09 (26.6%)**. In absolute terms, today’s heard count is the highest among those comparison dates, but it came against the largest listed docket as well, so throughput did not keep pace with filings listed for the day.

Among benches carrying meaningful volume, **E.S. Indiresh** stood out with **106 heard out of 145 scheduled (73.1%)**, while **V. Srishananda** disposed of **61 of 84 (72.6%)** and also cleared a small additional list fully. **Pradeep Singh Yerur** posted **45 of 65 (69.2%)**, and **K.S. Hemalekha** heard **48 of 74 (64.9%)**. At the heavier end of the board, **M. Nagaprasanna** heard **118 matters across 351 scheduled** lists, the highest raw disposal count in the dataset, though at a moderate conversion level. The weakest large-list conversion came from **S. Sunil Dutt Yadav**, whose combined lists yielded **33 hearings from 297 scheduled**, including two very large lists with **1 of 131** and **0 of 107** heard.

Stage movement favored earlier matters. **Admission (48.5%)** and **Preliminary Hearing (44.9%)** converted far better than **Final Hearing (21.5%)** and **Further Hearing (20.6%)**, suggesting stronger turnover in entry-stage work than in mature matters. By case type, **WP** dominated the docket with **1,669 scheduled** and **481 heard (28.8%)**; that rate was below **31.7% on 2026-04-17** and **33.7% on 2026-04-16**. **CRL.P** delivered **177 hearings from 540 listings (32.8%)**, while **MFA** was relatively stronger at **127 of 319 (39.8%)**. Smaller categories showed sharp clearance in **CMP (80.0%)**, **CRL.RP (77.5%)**, **RPFC (68.8%)**, and **CCC (66.7%)**, while **CRP**, **MFA.CROB**, **COM.OS**, and **RFA.CROB** saw no hearings at all.
